new_issues_created.description: SEC-4.5.4 HTML section says 'A local sun at fixed height H ≈ 8,537 km' — but 8,537 km is the firmament APEX HEIGHT, not the sun altitude. The dome's own core parameter is sun_altitude=5,733 km (from inject_ai_layer.py). DOCX already correctly says 5,733 km. A dome defender checking this would instantly spot the error.

SEC-4.5.3 HTML section says dome resolves gravity contradiction by 'using the globe formula g = GM / r²' — but the dome does not use this point-mass formula. It uses WGS84 empirical surface values (g = 9.7803 − 0.0325·cos(2φ)), which Section 4.5.8 correctly identifies. The wrong formula makes our argument attackable as a straw man.

Section numbering mismatch between HTML and DOCX in Part 4.5: HTML 4.5.3 = 'Gravity at the Rim', HTML 4.5.4 = 'Solar Diameter'. DOCX 4.5.3 = 'Solar Angular Diameter', DOCX 4.5.4 = 'Tidal Pattern'. Cross-format references in kill-shot table (row 11 references 'Section 4.5.3') are broken in one format.

internal_issues.description: SEC-4.5.4 uses firmament apex height (8,537 km) instead of sun altitude (5,733 km)

suggested_patch.find: A local sun at fixed height H ≈ 8,537 km, traveling in a circular path at latitude φ.
suggested_patch.replace: A local sun at fixed height H ≈ 5,733 km (the dome's own stated sun altitude, derived from crepuscular ray triangulation), traveling in a circular path at latitude φ.

internal_issues.description: SEC-4.5.3 cites wrong formula 'g = GM / r²' — dome uses WGS84 empirical values

suggested_patch.find: and instead using the globe formula g = GM / r².
suggested_patch.replace: and instead using the globe's empirically measured WGS84 surface gravity values (g ≈ 9.78–9.83 m/s², varying with latitude) — substituting globe data for his own model's prediction rather than deriving gravity from his aetheric circulation equations.

internal_issues.description: WIN-056 incorrectly listed in Section 4.9 aetheric refraction list; WIN-056 uses globe declination formula, not aetheric refraction

suggested_patch.find: At least six WINs rely on aetheric refraction to avoid falsification: WIN-016 (annual aberration), WIN-017 (stellar parallax), WIN-026 (crepuscular rays), WIN-033 (Sigma Octantis dimness), WIN-056 (solar elevation), WIN-065 (Polaris excess).
suggested_patch.replace: At least five WINs rely on aetheric refraction to avoid falsification: WIN-016 (annual aberration), WIN-017 (stellar parallax), WIN-026 (crepuscular rays), WIN-033 (Sigma Octantis dimness), WIN-065 (Polaris excess). WIN-056 uses a different evasion — the author substitutes the globe's 23.45° axial tilt declination formula rather than deriving solar elevation from dome geometry — and is catalogued in Section 4.5 (Self-Contradictions) rather than here.

internal_issues.description: P-wave shadow zone angular range written as '140-103°' (reversed) in kill-shot section; should be '104°-140°'

suggested_patch.find: The "P-wave shadow zone" (140–103° from epicenter) is caused by refraction at the liquid core boundary
suggested_patch.replace: The P-wave shadow zone (104°–140° from epicenter) is caused by refraction at the liquid core boundary

internal_issues.description: WIN-042 finding calls it 'Duplicate of WIN-037' — too dismissive; mathematical equivalence needs explanation

suggested_patch.find: Duplicate of WIN-037 with identical threshold and data
suggested_patch.replace: Mathematically equivalent to WIN-037 (≥28 nT cumulative per 12 months = ≥28 nT/yr rate); both WINs test SAA-core stations only, not the global dipole — see WIN-037 for full analysis

internal_issues.description: WIN-037 evidence never explicitly cross-references WIN-042's mathematical equivalence

suggested_patch.find: It was part of a batch of five predictions (WIN-035 through WIN-039) all registered on the same date, suggesting systematic data mining from already-published sources rather than prospective prediction.
suggested_patch.replace: It was part of a batch of five predictions (WIN-035 through WIN-039) all registered on the same date, suggesting systematic data mining from already-published sources rather than prospective prediction. WIN-042 makes the mathematically equivalent claim framed as an annualized rate (≥28 nT/yr) rather than a 12-month cumulative threshold — the same measurement expressed twice does not constitute two independent confirmations.

internal_issues.description: WIN-063 evidence doesn't preempt 'toroidal vortex naturally produces asymmetry' counter-argument

suggested_patch.find: However, no published formula connects this flow topology to a specific hemispheric decay asymmetry ratio.
suggested_patch.replace: However, no published formula connects this flow topology to a specific hemispheric decay asymmetry ratio — meaning a dome defender who argues 'our toroidal aetheric vortex naturally produces hemispheric asymmetry' has not addressed the core objection: without a predicted numerical value derived before measurement, any observed asymmetry ratio will retrospectively 'match' an unspecified topology. The dome needs to commit to a predicted ratio (e.g., NH decays 1.4× faster than SH over the next 10 years) before observation, not after.

internal_issues.description: WIN-047 doesn't use strongest Hubble argument: if aetheric redshift differs from expansion, dome must predict different H₀

suggested_patch.find: The dome has no ontology for extragalactic objects — without one, claims about their redshift behavior are physically meaningless.
suggested_patch.replace: The dome has no ontology for extragalactic objects — without one, claims about their redshift behavior are physically meaningless. A further problem: if aetheric propagation causes redshift, the dome must predict a DIFFERENT Hubble constant than standard cosmology — the value of H₀ would encode the aetheric attenuation rate per unit distance, not the cosmic expansion rate. The dome makes no such prediction, instead adopting the same H₀ range (67–74 km/s/Mpc) used by standard cosmology. If the mechanism differs but the prediction is identical, the observation confirms neither mechanism.
